Hencotes

---

HENCOTES = Henhouse   domestic hens.

The Place Names of Hexham Region	, Vic Watts, Master of Grey College, Durham.  13 Oct. 1992.
   1915. Hexham Urban Council's works and pleasure Grounds Committee agreed to widen the street at Hencotes.

Hex. Cour.	 6.3.2015   100 Years Ago.
   1919. Hexham Urban Council gave the go-ahead to plans to have Hencotes widened.

Hex. Cour.	 4.11.1994   75 Years Ago.
   1936. Looking back 30 years.  Development Scheme Advocated in 1936.  These sketches by Mr John E. Hedley, of Hexham, done 30 years ago, envisaged a Hexham of the future.  The sketch of Hencotes (centre), can be compared with the conditions as they exist today 1967].  The Town Development supplement issued by the "Hexham Courant" in March, 1936, showed that present-day planning schemes had been anticipated.

Hex. Cour.	 10.2.1967   to March 14th 1936.
   1956. A decision by the Ministry of Transport to allow parking on both sides of the A69 trunk road at Hencotes in Hexham was described as "a typical bit of administrative lunacy" by the vice-chairman of Hexham Urban Council, Coun. John Pickrering.

Hex. Cour.	 3.11.2006   50 Years Ago.
   1996. The "No Waiting" system on the A69 trunk road through Hexham should be extended so that it included the West End Inn at the junction of the Allendale Road, and the turning to Hextol Terrace, Hexham Urban District Council was told.

Hex. Cour.	 19.4.1991   25 Years Ago.
   1988. [...] the Presbytery, which belonged to the parish and has been lived in by a succession of Parish Priests and Assistants for almost two hundred years, was sold in November last year.  It was built by a Father Leadbitter O.P. (Dominican) in the 1790s to act as the residence for priests and a chapel where Mass could be provided for the local community.

Abbey Chronicle,	 Nov. 1989, p.1, Father Martin Deegan.
   2017. St. Cuthberts Close.  Demolition of existing two storey bedsit accommodation and pair of semi detached houses facing onto Hencotes and site regrading to suit proposed new build. 16/02439/FUL.  Construction of 38 elderly persons apartments accommodation to be provided in a two and three storey building with associated parking and landscaping. 16/03281/FUL Court House   1914. County Court (His Honor Francis George Greenwell M.A. judge; Lewis Chalmers Lockhart, registrar & high bailiff), Court house, Abbey buildings.

Kelly's Directory	, p.164.
   1929. County Court (His Honor Sir Fras. Jn. Greenwell C.B.E. M.A. judge; Alfd. Darby Minton-Senhouse, registrar & high bailiff), Court ho. Hencotes.

Kelly's Directory	, p.84.
   1934. County Court (His Honour Arth. Lionel Bruce Thesiger. judge; Alfd. Darby Minton-Senhouse, registrar & high bailiff), Court ho. Hencotes.

Kelly's Directory	, p.100.
   1938. County Court Offices (His Honour Thomas Richardson O.B.E.,  LL.B. judge; Alfd. Darby Minton-Senhouse, registrar & high bailiff), Market pl.

Kelly's Directory	, p.105.
 Cock Fighting at No.43   1835. The bloodsport has been banned for nearly 200 years but has had a recent revival.  The trend is being driven by Asian and traveller minorities for whom putting cockerels against one another, often to the death, is still [in 2017] culturally accepted. [...] Cockfighting is a brutal and barbaric bloodsport which sees cockerels inflicting horrendous injuries on each other, often fighting to the death.  It was outlawed in 1835 but, sadly, it hasn't gone away. [...] Cockfighters are very secretive so it can be extremely difficult to infiltrate and investigste effectively.

The Times	, 22.12.2017.
   1866. Eminent word-sleuth Michael Quinton says that originally it [long-heeled] had no connection with wealth or even dress sense.  His source   though not acknowledged by the OED
   was cockfighting.  Cockfighting was   and still is where it continues   a nasty sport, with much blood and flying of feathers, particularly since the cockerel combatants were equipped with sharpened spurs to add potency to their blows.  When conducted without these extra weapons, it is called naked-heel fighting, and is an even more drawn-out business.  So, the theory goes, if you attached a spur or heel to your cockerel, you were at an advantage, though there can surely not have been a game in which both birds were not equally equipped.  From this practice, at any rate, comes the expression 'well heeled', first spotted in print in a story   again by Mark Twain   from 1866.  Nowadays someone described as well heeled can be either 'well off' or 'well dressed', and few people are aware of the phrase's bloody origins.

Balderdash & Piffle.  One Sandwich Short of a Dog's Dinner	, p.48, Alex Games, BBC Books, 2007.
   2004. HENCOTES. A previous owner of Laburnum Cottage, 43 Hencotes, said that she had been told that fights were held in the loft with a semi-circular window, at the west end of the building above the trap house.  When the police [bailiffs ?] came they would go in the front door, up the stairs and turn left in to a room.  As there was a cupboard door and a room door the layout of that room was confusing.  The cock pit was through a door and down two steps into the loft.  When the bailiff arrived the cocks would be put down through a trapdoor into the trap house below, let out through a door which then was just wide enough for a pony, then across the garden and out onto the Seal.  The building with pantiles against the Seal wall was the house for the man looking after the pony and trap, a one up and one down.  She and her husband had removed the metal bars over the D shaped window facing Hencotes because they rattled in the wind.  The trapdoor was still in the floor but now under a carpet.

Pers. mems. Mrs. Usher, now of Corbridge, 1 Mar. 2004.
 Listed Buildings, 1948 Provisional List of Buildings of Architectural or Historic Interest, 1948. South side   2/90. Grade II. Burn Brae House.  Late C.18 brick, porch added later.     3 storeys, 3 windows.  Has been much added to, and altered at the back,     but elevation to the street is intact.   2/91. Grade III. Disused Scotch Church.  Stone, dated 1825.  2 storeys,     3 window facade with pediment and simple doorway with plain square-faced pilasters.     Interesting as example of style of Scotch churches of the period.     Inscription on pediment:  "Scotch Church, 1825".   The following six houses Nos. 12 to 22 (even Nos.), form a grade II group.     The individual houses would in no case be graded so highly if standing alone.     All are early C.19.   2/92. Grade II. No. 12.  Stucco, 2 storeys, 3 windows.   2/93. Grade II. No. 14.  Stucco, 3 storeys, 2 windows.  (2/94. Grade II. No. 16.  Stone, 3 storeys, 2 windows.  (2/95. Grade II. No. 18.   2/96. Grade II. No. 20 (Hencotes House).  Stone, 2 storeys, 5 windows.   2/97. Grade II. No. 22 (later demolished).  Stone, 3 storeys, 2 windows,     with small annexe 2 storeys, 1 window. North side  (2/98. Grade II. No. 23.  Built as one house.  Late C.18 or early C.19 brick.  3 storeys,  (2/99. Grade II. No. 25.  2 windows, with blank window centre on 1st and second floors.   2/100. Grade II. No. 35.  Early C.19 stone, 2 storeys, 3 windows.   2/101. Grade II. No. 37.  Late C.18 or early C.19 stone.  3 storeys, 1 window.   2/102. Grade II. No. 43 Laburnum Cottage. Late C.18 or early C.19 brick.     3 storeys, 4 windows (1 blank).  Porch added later.   1951.  Roman Catholic Presbytery.

Statutory List of Buildings of Special Architectural or Historic Interest.

1976.  St. Mary's R.C.Church (Grade II*).  All Grade II: Gateway to R.C.Cemetery     from Gaprigg Court, Catholic Club, Nos. 6, 8, 1, 3 & 5, 15, 17, 39, 39A, 43. 19A Hencotes   2018. Mr Matt Collis, change of use from A1 to C3.  Conversion of shop to one bedroom residential unit, Sisterson News, 19A Hencotes, NE46 2EQ, ref. no: 18/03273/COU. Hex. Cour.	 4.10.2018, p.25 & p.64, Planning applications.
 23 Hencotes   1948. Grade II.  No. 23, No.25.  Built as one house.  Late C.18 or early C.19 brick.  3 storeys, 2 windows, with blank window centre on 1st and second floors.

Provisional List of Buildings of Architectural or Historic Interest for Consideration in connection with the Provisions of Section 30 of the Town and Country Planning Act, 1947.  Provisional List, September 1948.
   1951. No. on the map 1/58 & 1/59.  Nos. 23 and 25.  Miss A. Stainthorpe. Owner. Planning Act 1947.  Statutory List, 2nd October 1951.
   1990. Hexham Town Scheme.  Schedule of Properties: Hencotes No. 23.   1991. Hencotes No.23.  Nos. 627-640: Tatman,Charles C, James C, Katharine A, Timothy C.

Register of Electors, Hencotes Ward.
   No. 2/88.  Nos. 23 and 25.  Grade II.

List of Properties.
   1992. Hexham Town Scheme: An arrangement for providing financial assistance to encourage the conservation and restoration of historic properties within a Conservationn Area.  Schedule of Buildings: Hencotes 23 and 25.   2001. Hencotes No.23.  Nos. 219-220: Tatman, Karen G, Timothy C.

Register of Electors, Hencotes Ward.
   2017. To the left of the front door, which is under a central window, there is an outline of another doorcase.

Tim Tatman, owner, 22.8.2017.
   Carved stone head.  Built into the rear wall of the house, about 3m from the ground, facing north.  Carved stone statuette standing in the garden.29 Hencotes   2018. Edward Hewitt, change of use from vacant shop from A1 (shop) to C3a (dwelling house), incorporating the shop room into living space for the existing dwelling, 29 Hencotes, ref. no: 18/01734/COU.

Hex. Cour.	 7.6.2018, p.25, Planning Applications.
   Ref. No. 18/01734/COU  29 Hencotes Hexham NE46 2EQ

Hex. Cour.	 14.6.2018, p.63.
 T. A. Drill Hall   1883. Northumberland Fusiliers held their annual Christmas shooting competition at the Highwood rifle range, near Hexham.

Hex. Cour.	 24.11.2008   125 Years Ago.
   1914. The Territorial Force stationed in Hexham comprise the head quarters of the D Squadron of the Northumberland (Hussars) Yeomanry, and the 4th Battalion the Northumberland Fusiliers and A Company of the Regiment.  A drill hall is now (1914) in course of erection in Hencotes.

Kelly's Directory,	 p.159.
   Hexham's Army drill hall in Hencotes opened.

Hex. Cour.	 15.5.1998.
   1998. Hexham's Territorial Army centre was saved from closure, after a surprise U-turn on a programme of defence cuts.

Hex. Cour.	 14.11.2008   10 Years Ago.
   2004. Northumbria Army Cadet Force have taken charge of a new facility in Hexham, with the refurbishment of the old Territorial Army firing range.

Hex. Cour.	 16.1.2004.
 Jackman Barracks   2013. After a 14-year absence the Fusiliers are moving back into Hexham.  About 20 members of the Royal Regiment of Fusiliers will be making the town their home over the coming year.  The Army reserves took the keys to the TA Centre earlier this month.  [...]  From January it will also have a new name   the Jackman Barracks.

Hex. Cour.	 27.12.2013, p.5.
 Burnbrae House, Hencotes 1846. Burn Brae Cottage, Maltkiln.   Above the lintel of the door facing south, a stone panel with a shield, the field	 divided fesswise, the chief	 is plain (for argent	   silver?) and the base	 hatched vertically (for gules	   red?).  In the corners of the panel, outside the shield, are raised carvings: at the top    J    B  at the bottom  18    46. 1847. Date stone J.B / 1847  Raised, on the keystone of third-floor window, facing east. 1860. Braeburn Ho

O.S. map, 1st ed.
 1896. Burn Brae House.

O.S. map, 2nd ed.
 1937. A fire, which broke out in Burn Brae House, Hexham, was prevented from becoming more serious by the prompt action of a passer-by.  The fire was believed to have been started by an iron that had been accidentally switched on.

Hex. Cour.	 10.2.2012   75 Years Ago.
 1957. A nice variety of minor Georgian buildings.  First BARN BRAE HOUSE   with tripartite windows, but on the narrow side to the E two Venetiean windows and a tripartite lunette on top of each other.

Buildings of England, Northumberland	, Pevsner, 1957, p.183.
 1992. Minor Georgian buildings.  First BURN BRAE HOUSE, brick with tripartite windows and, in the gable end, two Venetiean windows with a lunette above.

Buildings of England, Northumberland	, Pevsner, 1992, p.332.
 1822. Gentry & Clergy.  Fell Mrs. Mary, Burn brae house, Hencoates street.

Pigot's Directory for Northumberland	, 1822, p.582.

   Stephenson Wm. Jn. chemical manure mnfr. Beaumont st

Kelly's Directory	, p.107.
 1939. McGill, Charles;  McGill, Ann;  McGill, John;  McGill, Polly;  Dickson, Robert Alexander McKinnel; Surgery, Burn Brae (Abode   Westacres).

Register of Electors, 1939, Hencotes Ward, p.10.
   Lynch, Ellen;  Lynch, James;  Clapperton Robert;  Currie, Jessie Morrison.  Burnbrae Cottage.

Register of Electors, 1939.
 2007.BURN BRAE MEDICAL GROUP   Dr. Tim A. Carney, Dr. Celia D. Helliwell, Dr. F. M.  Noreen Powell, Dr. Eleanor I. Gallagher, Dr. David A. Shovlin, Dr. Paul McClintock.

Signboard outside the Hencotes surgery, April 2007.
 2008. Hexham's GPs are preparing to move into their fresh premises in the new £23 million wing of Hexham General Hospital.  The Burn Brae and Selegate practices get the keys to the door next week, and will begin moving fittings and fixtures across the following week.

Hex. Cour.	 2.5.2008.
 Selegate House   1886. Greenhow Miss Mary, Sealgate house.

Bulmer's Directory	, p.356.
   1914. No entry found.   1928. The Hexham Orthopaedic Clinic, opened at Cotfield in 1918, for the treatment of disabled soldiers, moved to its fine new premises at Selegate House, Hexham.

Hex. Cour. 2.5.2003   75 Years Ago.
   1929. Hexham Orthopædic Annexe (E. Bowering Jones, sec.), Hencotes.  TN 167.

Kelly's Directory	, p.86.
   1934. Hexham Orthopædic Clinic (E. Bowering Jones, sec.), Sele Gate ho. Hencotes.  TN 167.

Kelly's Directory	, p.101.
   1938. Hexham Orthopædic Clinic (E. Bowering Jones, sec.), Sele Gate ho. Hencotes.  TN 167.

Kelly's Directory	, p.106.
   2008. Hexham's GPs are preparing to move into their fresh premises in the new £23 million wing of Hexham General Hospital.  The Burn Brae and Selegate practices get the keys to the door next week, and will begin moving fittings and fixtures across the following week.

Hex. Cour.	 2.5.2008.
   2011. In September the frontage was altered and a wooden door put in place. Hencotes Mews   1860. The O.S. map shows long gardens to the north of the properties on Hencotes, reaching as far as the Seal.  A wall at the east side of where no. 33 is now, was the west wall of the garden of Seal Terrace.   1960s. The O.S. map shows the wall to the west of Sele House	 with a large Garage	 shown in the northern half of the garden of 31 Hencotes.  The access to the car repair garage was through the (still existing) narrow arched entrance.   1929. Forster & Low Ltd., motor engineers, were at no. 33 in 1929.  They had a car showroom at the front, to the west of the archway, vehicles being pushed across the pavement through large opening glazed doors.  There was a petrol pump outside which had a swivelling arm so that vehicles could be filled up whilst standing on the road.   1939. Purvis, Ada Maud & Purvis, Norman are in a shop at 33 Hencotes.  there 7 residents shown at Seal House	, and 2 at Seal Cottage.

Register of Electors, p.10.
   1960s. Miss Low was living in Sele House.    1970s. Foster & Low was bought and demolished by Bishops Garages Ltd. in the late 70s or early 80s.  Bishops Garages bought Hexham Motor Company on Battle Hill and demolished it in 1974 and opened up a garage in Alemouth Road.   1988. Hencotes Mews is shown on the  O.S.Map.

---

CND 4.1,  4 Oct 2018
